required.title: [Listen] Native speaker recordings for target language

suggested.title: [Listen] Podcasts or audio content in target language

supplementary.title: [Reference] WALS (World Atlas of Language Structures, wals.info)

competence_markers.description: The student can read and write IPA transcriptions of English words and target language words with reasonable accuracy
The student can describe any consonant by its place, manner, and voicing using standard phonological terminology
The student can map the vowel system of their target language onto the IPA vowel chart and compare it to English
The student understands the phoneme/allophone distinction and can give examples from at least two languages
The student can identify regular sound correspondences across related languages using the comparative method
The student can explain Grimm's Law with at least five specific cognate examples
The student can describe the major branches of at least two language families with their key phonological features
The student engages in daily shadowing practice and can demonstrate measurable improvement in target language pronunciation over the course of the packet

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain the IPA. What problem does it solve? How is it organized?
narration_prompts.detail: Pick a consonant that does not exist in English and describe it using place, manner, and voicing. Can you produce it?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between a phoneme and an allophone?
narration_prompts.detail: Give an example from English and an example from another language. Why does this distinction matter for language learners?
What did Grimm discover? Give at least three examples of the sound correspondences. What does this systematic pattern prove?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe the vowel system of your target language.
narration_prompts.detail: How many vowels does it have? Where do they sit in the vowel space? How does this compare to English? Which vowels are hardest for you and why?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a language family other than Indo-European.
narration_prompts.detail: What languages are in it? Where are they spoken? What are their distinctive phonological features? What evidence connects them as a family?

What are you noticing about the target language's sound system through daily practice? What sounds have gotten easier? What is still difficult? How does your recording from this week compare to your first recording?

cross_connections.description: The structural typology developed in 3.1.1-B directly complements the phonological typology here. The student who can classify a language by word order type and morphological type (3.1.1) and by its sound system (3.1.4) has a two-dimensional map of any language's architecture.
The IPA learned here connects to script literacy: the student can now see the gap between how a language is written and how it is pronounced, and can use IPA to bridge that gap for any script.

The pronunciation skills developed here directly serve vocabulary acquisition in 3.1.3. The student who can analyze and reproduce target language sounds will retain vocabulary more accurately and be understood more consistently.

Grimm's Law and the comparative method connect language change to historical events -- the Indo-European expansion that 1.1.1 covers from the civilizational side. Language evidence and archaeological evidence tell the same story from different angles.
The IPA and phonological analysis are applied science -- precise observation, systematic classification, and hypothesis testing applied to speech sounds. The methods mirror scientific methodology.
Daily shadowing practice is a discipline commitment. The student who maintains consistent practice builds the habit structure that serves every domain.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you discovered that the world's languages are organized into families and that human speech uses an astonishing range of sounds -- clicks, tones, trills, and sounds made in parts of the throat you did not know you had. You trained your ears to listen and your mouth to experiment.

Now you are going to acquire the tools that transform listening and mimicry into systematic capability. You are going to learn the International Phonetic Alphabet -- a notation system that can represent every sound in every human language with precision. You are going to learn to describe any consonant by exactly where and how it is made in your mouth. You are going to map vowel space. You are going to study the major language families in real depth -- not just their names and locations, but the sound correspondences that prove they are related and the systematic sound shifts that drove them apart.

Most importantly, you are going to learn to apply all of this to actual language acquisition. The IPA is not an academic exercise. It is the tool that lets you look at any word in any language and know exactly how to pronounce it -- even if you have never heard it spoken. The phonological knowledge you build here is the single most powerful accelerant of language learning that exists, because pronunciation is where most learners fail, and pronunciation is exactly what this packet trains.

By the end of this packet, when you encounter a new language, you will not guess at its sounds. You will analyze them. You will know exactly what your mouth needs to do. And you will do it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)** as a practical tool for transcribing and producing the sounds of any language
2. **Systematic consonant analysis** -- the ability to describe any consonant by its place, manner, and voicing
3. **Vowel space mapping** -- understanding where all human vowels are produced and how to navigate between them
4. **The phoneme/allophone distinction** -- understanding why speakers of different languages literally cannot hear certain sound differences
5. **Deep knowledge of major language families** -- the Indo-European family tree in detail, plus systematic knowledge of at least three other families
6. **The comparative method** -- the ability to identify sound correspondences across related languages and understand what they reveal about language history
7. **Immersion techniques** -- practical methods for acquiring the sound system of a target language through systematic listening and production practice
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The International Phonetic Alphabet -- Your Universal Key

In 1888, a group of linguists created a system that could write down the sounds of any human language using a single, consistent set of symbols. They called it the International Phonetic Alphabet -- the IPA.

The problem they were solving is real: English spelling is a disaster for pronunciation. The letters "ough" are pronounced differently in "though," "through," "thought," "tough," "cough," and "bough." The letter "c" sounds like "k" in "cat" but "s" in "city." If you see a word written in English, you often cannot be certain how to say it. And English is not even the worst offender -- French, Irish, and Tibetan all have enormous gaps between spelling and pronunciation.

The IPA eliminates this problem. Each symbol represents exactly one sound, and each sound is represented by exactly one symbol. If you can read IPA, you can pronounce any word in any language -- even one you have never heard spoken. This is not an exaggeration. It is the reason linguists and speech pathologists and language teachers around the world use the IPA as their standard tool.

You already know some IPA symbols without realizing it. Many of them look like familiar letters:
- [p] = the "p" in "pin"
- [b] = the "b" in "bin"
- [t] = the "t" in "tin"
- [d] = the "d" in "din"
- [k] = the "k" in "kin"
- [m] = the "m" in "moon"
- [n] = the "n" in "noon"
- [s] = the "s" in "sun"
- [z] = the "z" in "zoo"

But others represent sounds that English does not spell clearly:
- [ʃ] = the "sh" in "ship" (English needs two letters for one sound; IPA needs one)
- [tʃ] = the "ch" in "chip"
- [ŋ] = the "ng" in "sing" (a single sound that English writes with two letters)
- [θ] = the "th" in "think"
- [ð] = the "th" in "this" (a different sound from "think" -- notice!)
- [ə] = the unstressed vowel in "about" or "sofa" (the most common vowel in English, and it has no dedicated letter in English spelling)

And some IPA symbols represent sounds that do not exist in English at all:
- [x] = the "ch" in Scottish "loch" or German "Bach"
- [ʁ] = the French "r"
- [ɣ] = a voiced velar fricative, found in Arabic, Spanish, and Greek
- [ʔ] = the glottal stop -- the sound in the middle of "uh-oh" (you make this sound constantly in English but have no letter for it)

Learning the IPA is not about memorizing every symbol at once. It is about understanding the system that generates the symbols -- because the IPA is not arbitrary. Every symbol is placed on a chart organized by exactly how the sound is made. Learn the chart, and the symbols make sense.

### Part 2: Consonants -- Place, Manner, and Voicing

Every consonant in every human language can be described using three parameters:

**Place of articulation** -- where in the mouth the sound is made:
- **Bilabial:** Both lips together -- [p], [b], [m]
- **Labiodental:** Lower lip against upper teeth -- [f], [v]
- **Dental:** Tongue against teeth -- [θ] (think), [ð] (this)
- **Alveolar:** Tongue against the ridge behind your upper teeth -- [t], [d], [n], [s], [z], [l]
- **Postalveolar:** Tongue just behind the alveolar ridge -- [ʃ] (ship), [ʒ] (measure)
- **Retroflex:** Tongue curled back -- found in Hindi, Mandarin, and many other languages; not standard in English
- **Palatal:** Tongue against the hard palate -- [j] (the "y" in "yes")
- **Velar:** Tongue against the soft palate (velum) -- [k], [g], [ŋ] (sing)
- **Uvular:** Tongue against the uvula -- the French "r" [ʁ], Arabic "q" [q]
- **Pharyngeal:** Constriction in the throat -- Arabic [ħ] and [ʕ], sounds most English speakers have never consciously produced
- **Glottal:** At the vocal folds themselves -- [h], [ʔ] (glottal stop)

**Manner of articulation** -- how the airflow is modified:
- **Stop (plosive):** Complete blockage then release -- [p], [b], [t], [d], [k], [g]
- **Fricative:** Narrow channel creating friction -- [f], [v], [s], [z], [ʃ], [h]
- **Affricate:** A stop immediately followed by a fricative -- [tʃ] (chip), [dʒ] (judge)
- **Nasal:** Air flows through the nose -- [m], [n], [ŋ]
- **Lateral:** Air flows around the sides of the tongue -- [l]
- **Trill:** Rapid vibration -- [r] (Spanish "rr" in "perro")
- **Tap/flap:** Single quick contact -- [ɾ] (the "t" in American English "butter")
- **Approximant:** Narrowing without friction -- [w], [j] (yes), [ɹ] (English "r")

**Voicing** -- whether the vocal folds vibrate:
- **Voiceless:** No vibration -- [p], [t], [k], [f], [s]
- **Voiced:** Vocal folds vibrate -- [b], [d], [g], [v], [z]

Put your hand on your throat and say "ssssss." Now say "zzzzzz." Feel the difference? The [z] vibrates; the [s] does not. Same place, same manner, different voicing. That is the voicing distinction.

With these three parameters, you can describe any consonant in any language. The Spanish trilled "r" is a voiced alveolar trill. The Arabic "q" is a voiceless uvular stop. The Zulu click in "Xhosa" is a voiceless lateral click. Once you know the system, the description tells you exactly what your mouth needs to do.

### Part 3: Vowels -- The Vowel Space

Consonants are made by obstructing airflow somewhere in the mouth. Vowels are different -- the air flows freely, and the sound is shaped by the position of the tongue and the shape of the lips.

The IPA vowel chart maps a space defined by two dimensions:
- **Height:** How high or low is the tongue? (High vowels like [i] in "see" versus low vowels like [a] in "father")
- **Backness:** Is the tongue pushed forward or pulled back? (Front vowels like [i] in "see" versus back vowels like [u] in "blue")

A third feature modifies this:
- **Rounding:** Are the lips rounded? ([u] in "blue" is rounded; [i] in "see" is unrounded)

English has a complex vowel system -- roughly twelve or more distinct vowel sounds, depending on dialect. This is actually unusual. Many languages have far simpler systems:
- **Arabic** has three basic vowels: [a], [i], [u] -- low, front-high, back-high. Three corners of the vowel space, and that is enough.
- **Spanish** has five vowels: [a], [e], [i], [o], [u] -- clean, consistent, and each one sounds the same every time it appears. This is why Spanish pronunciation is considered easy for English speakers -- not because the sounds are simple, but because they are consistent.
- **English** has twelve-plus vowels crammed into the same space, many of them very close together. This is why English pronunciation is difficult for learners -- the differences between "bit," "bet," "bat," and "but" are tiny acoustic differences that English speakers learn to hear as children but that speakers of five-vowel languages struggle to distinguish.

Understanding where your target language's vowels sit in the vowel space -- and how that space compares to English's -- is one of the most powerful tools for improving your pronunciation.

### Part 4: Phonemes and Allophones -- Why You Cannot Hear What You Cannot Hear

Here is one of the most important concepts in phonology: **the sounds that matter in one language may be invisible in another.**

In English, the [p] in "pin" and the [p] in "spin" are physically different sounds. The first one has a puff of air after it (aspirated: [pʰ]). The second does not (unaspirated: [p]). Hold your hand in front of your mouth and say both words. You will feel the puff of air on "pin" but not on "spin."

English speakers do not notice this difference. They hear both sounds as "p." That is because in English, aspiration does not change meaning -- "pin" with aspiration and "pin" without aspiration are the same word. These two sounds are **allophones** of the same **phoneme** /p/.

But in Hindi, aspiration changes meaning. The Hindi word [pal] means "moment." The Hindi word [pʰal] means "fruit." Different phonemes. Different words. A Hindi speaker hears the difference as clearly as you hear the difference between "b" and "p." And an English speaker, trying to learn Hindi, literally cannot hear the difference at first -- because their brain has been trained since infancy to ignore it.

This is the phoneme/allophone distinction, and it explains the single largest challenge in language acquisition: **your native language has trained you to hear certain sound differences and to be deaf to others.** The Japanese speaker who says "rice" and "lice" as the same word is not being careless -- in Japanese, [r] and [l] are allophones of the same phoneme. The distinction is literally inaudible to them without specific training.

The good news: this perceptual deafness can be overcome. It takes targeted practice -- listening to minimal pairs (words that differ by only one sound), getting feedback, and training the ear to hear a distinction it has been ignoring. This is exactly what immersion techniques are designed to do.

### Part 5: The Indo-European Family in Depth

You learned in Stretching Branches that Indo-European is a language family stretching from Iceland to Sri Lanka. Now you are going to see the internal structure -- the branches and sub-branches -- and the sound correspondences that prove the family relationship.

**The major branches:**
- **Germanic:** English, German, Dutch, Swedish, Norwegian, Danish, Icelandic
- **Romance:** French, Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Romanian (all descended from Latin)
- **Slavic:** Russian, Polish, Czech, Ukrainian, Serbian, Croatian, Bulgarian
- **Celtic:** Irish, Welsh, Scottish Gaelic, Breton
- **Indo-Iranian:** Hindi, Urdu, Bengali, Farsi, Kurdish, Pashto
- **Baltic:** Lithuanian, Latvian
- **Hellenic:** Greek
- **Albanian:** Albanian
- **Armenian:** Armenian

**Grimm's Law -- the smoking gun.** In 1822, Jacob Grimm (yes, the fairy tale collector) described a systematic sound shift that separated the Germanic languages from the rest of Indo-European. The pattern:

Where Latin (and other non-Germanic IE languages) has [p], Germanic languages have [f]:
- Latin *pater* -- English *father*, German *Vater*
- Latin *piscis* -- English *fish*
- Latin *pes* -- English *foot*

Where Latin has [t], Germanic has [θ] (the "th" sound):
- Latin *tres* -- English *three*
- Latin *tenuis* -- English *thin*

Where Latin has [k], Germanic has [h]:
- Latin *centum* -- English *hundred*
- Latin *cor* -- English *heart*

This is not coincidence. It is a systematic sound change that affected every word in the language, following a precise phonological rule. Grimm's Law is one of the foundational discoveries of historical linguistics -- and it is the proof that these languages are related.

### Part 6: Beyond Indo-European -- Three More Families

**The Afroasiatic Family:**
Stretching across North Africa and the Middle East, Afroasiatic includes Arabic, Hebrew, Amharic, Hausa, and Berber languages. These languages share a distinctive feature: the root-and-pattern morphological system. In Arabic, the consonants k-t-b carry the core meaning "write." Changing the vowel pattern around those consonants generates an entire family of related words: *kitab* (book), *kataba* (he wrote), *kaatib* (writer), *maktaba* (library). This system is shared across the family and is unlike anything in Indo-European.

The Afroasiatic sound systems include pharyngeal and emphatic consonants that most Indo-European languages lack entirely. Arabic's [ʕ] (ayn) and [ħ] (ḥa) are produced deep in the throat -- sounds that represent some of the most challenging acquisitions for English speakers.

**The Niger-Congo Family:**
The largest language family by number of languages, Niger-Congo includes over 1,500 languages across sub-Saharan Africa. The Bantu sub-family alone includes Swahili, Zulu, Xhosa, Shona, and hundreds more. Niger-Congo languages are overwhelmingly tonal, often with complex tone systems that interact with grammar (tone can mark tense, aspect, or grammatical relationships, not just lexical meaning). Many Niger-Congo languages also have extensive noun class systems -- like grammatical gender but with far more categories (Swahili has over fifteen noun classes).

**The Sino-Tibetan Family:**
Home to the world's most spoken language (Mandarin), Sino-Tibetan spans China, Tibet, Burma, and parts of Southeast Asia. Sino-Tibetan languages are tonal, often analytic (relying on word order rather than inflection), and many are written with Chinese characters or derivatives. Cantonese's six tones, Mandarin's four tones plus neutral, and the complex tone sandhi rules (where tones change based on adjacent tones) make Sino-Tibetan phonology a rich and challenging field.

### Part 7: Sound Correspondences and the Comparative Method

The comparative method is the detective work of historical linguistics. Given words from related languages, you identify systematic sound correspondences -- places where one language consistently has one sound where another language consistently has a different sound -- and use those correspondences to reconstruct the ancestor language.

Here is a worked example with the Romance languages (descendants of Latin):

The word for "night":
- Latin: *noctem*
- Spanish: *noche*
- French: *nuit*
- Italian: *notte*
- Portuguese: *noite*
- Romanian: *noapte*

The Latin [kt] cluster evolved differently in each language: Spanish [tʃ], French (lost entirely), Italian [tt], Portuguese [it], Romanian [pt]. Each change is systematic -- it happens the same way in every word with the same cluster, not just this one.

When you find these regular correspondences, you have evidence of genetic relationship. When you trace them backward, you can reconstruct features of the ancestor language. This is how linguists reconstructed Proto-Indo-European -- a language nobody ever recorded, but whose features we can infer from its descendants.

### Part 8: Immersion Techniques for Sound Acquisition

Knowing the IPA and understanding phonological theory is necessary but not sufficient. You must train your ear and your mouth through immersion in real speech. Here are the methods that work:

**Shadowing:** Listen to a native speaker and repeat what they say in real time, as closely as you can, with as little delay as possible. You are not trying to understand -- you are trying to reproduce the sounds, rhythm, and intonation. Start with short, simple phrases and build to longer passages. This technique, used consistently, rewires your articulatory habits faster than any other method.

**Minimal pair drilling:** Identify the sound contrasts that are hardest for you in your target language (the ones that are allophones in English but phonemes in the target language). Find minimal pairs -- words that differ only in that one sound. Listen to them repeatedly. Try to identify which is which. Produce them and get feedback from a native speaker or recording. This is targeted training for your perceptual system.

**Prosodic mimicry:** Before you worry about individual sounds, capture the overall music of the language -- its rhythm, its stress pattern, its intonation contours. Mimic entire sentences using nonsense syllables that preserve the prosody. A learner who has the rhythm right and some sounds wrong will be more intelligible than a learner who has the sounds right but the rhythm wrong.

**Recording and comparison:** Record yourself producing target language phrases. Play your recording alongside a native speaker recording. Listen for differences. This self-monitoring builds awareness of your own production errors that you cannot detect in real time.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: IPA Bootcamp [First Three Weeks]
Learn the IPA systematically. Week 1: consonants organized by place and manner. Week 2: vowels organized by height, backness, and rounding. Week 3: transcription practice -- transcribe English words into IPA, then read IPA transcriptions of target language words and produce them. By the end of three weeks, the student should be able to read any IPA transcription and approximate the pronunciation.

### Study 2: Consonant Mapping [Early Packet]
For the student's target language, produce a complete consonant inventory chart: every consonant the language uses, placed by place, manner, and voicing. Compare this chart to English. Identify: (a) sounds shared with English, (b) sounds the target language has that English lacks, and (c) sounds English has that the target language lacks. Category (b) is the student's priority list for production practice.

### Study 3: Vowel Space Comparison [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Map the vowel systems of English and the target language onto the IPA vowel chart. Identify where they overlap and where they diverge. For each target-language vowel that does not exist in English, describe its position (height, backness, rounding) and practice producing it in isolation and in words.

### Study 4: The Phoneme Hunt [Mid-Packet]
Identify at least three pairs of sounds that are allophones in English but phonemes in the target language (or vice versa). For each pair, find minimal pairs in the target language, practice discrimination (can you hear the difference?), and practice production (can you make the difference?). Write up your findings: what are the sounds, why are they difficult, and what is your strategy for mastering them?

### Study 5: Language Family Deep Dive [Mid-Packet]
Choose one language family other than Indo-European. Map its branches, major languages, geographic distribution, and key phonological features. Present the cognate evidence that proves the family relationship. Written presentation of at least 500 words plus a map.

### Study 6: Grimm's Law Demonstration [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Collect at least ten cognate sets that demonstrate Grimm's Law (the p/f, t/th, k/h correspondences between Latin and English). For each set, identify the correspondence, transcribe the relevant sounds in IPA, and explain why this systematic pattern proves genetic relationship rather than coincidence.

### Study 7: Shadowing Practice [Ongoing from Mid-Packet]
Begin daily shadowing practice with target language audio. Start with 5 minutes per day and build to 15-20 minutes. The student shadows native speech in real time, focusing on reproducing rhythm, intonation, and individual sounds. Record yourself weekly and compare to earlier recordings to track improvement.

### Study 8: Sound Correspondence Exercise [Late Packet]
Given a set of cognates from a language family the student has not yet studied, identify the regular sound correspondences. This is the comparative method in practice. The student demonstrates that they can apply the analytical tools to new data, not just recite what they have learned about Indo-European.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. IPA Proficiency
**Developing well:** The student can transcribe common English words in IPA with few errors and can read IPA transcriptions of target language words to produce reasonable approximations. They use the IPA chart as a reference tool naturally -- looking up unfamiliar symbols when they encounter them.

**Developing slowly:** The student confuses IPA symbols with English letters, cannot transcribe without heavy assistance, or treats IPA as an abstract exercise disconnected from actual pronunciation.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting enough transcription practice? IPA proficiency comes from repetition, not from understanding the concept once. Are they connecting IPA transcriptions to actual listening and production, or just doing written exercises?

### 2. Consonant and Vowel Analysis
**Developing well:** The student can describe consonants using place, manner, and voicing terminology and can locate vowels in the vowel space. When encountering a new sound in their target language, they analyze it systematically: "That sounds like a voiced velar fricative -- let me check the IPA chart."

**Developing slowly:** The student memorizes descriptions but cannot apply them to new sounds. They treat the terminology as vocabulary to be recited rather than tools to be used.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you presenting new sounds for the student to analyze, or only asking them to recall analyses you have taught them? The skill is analysis, not memory.

### 3. Phoneme/Allophone Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain the distinction clearly with examples from multiple languages. They can predict which target language contrasts will be hardest for them based on which contrasts are allophonic in English.

**Developing slowly:** The student can recite the definition but cannot apply it. They do not see why it matters for their own language learning.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you given the student the experience of not hearing a contrast? Play target language minimal pairs and ask them to distinguish. If they cannot, that lived experience teaches the concept better than any explanation.

### 4. Comparative Method Application
**Developing well:** The student can identify regular sound correspondences in cognate sets from language families they have studied and can apply the method to new data. They understand that regular correspondence proves relationship while random similarity does not.

**Developing slowly:** The student can recognize Grimm's Law examples but cannot identify correspondences in unfamiliar data. They confuse coincidental similarity with systematic correspondence.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had enough practice with varied data? Give them cognate sets from a family they have not studied and see if they can find the pattern independently.

### 5. Shadowing Improvement
**Developing well:** Comparison of early and late recordings shows clear improvement in rhythm, intonation, and individual sound accuracy. The student can identify specific improvements and specific ongoing challenges in their own production.

**Developing slowly:** Little change between early and late recordings. The student may be going through the motions without engaging their articulatory system fully.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student shadowing with focused attention or while multitasking? Is the audio material appropriate -- clear, not too fast, with good recording quality? Is the student getting any feedback (from native speakers, from self-recording comparison)?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### IPA as Busywork
If transcription exercises feel like fill-in-the-blank worksheets, the method has been lost. Every transcription should connect to a real pronunciation goal. "Transcribe these ten English words" is a warm-up exercise, not a study. "Transcribe the IPA for these target language words, then produce them, then check against a native speaker recording" is a study.

### Skipping the Experiential Component
The student who understands phonological theory but does not practice shadowing, minimal pairs, and production drills has acquired knowledge without capability. This packet demands both. If you must choose, prioritize the practice over the theory -- a student who shadows daily but cannot define "alveolar fricative" is better prepared for language acquisition than one who can define every term but has not trained their ear.

### Under-Covering Non-Indo-European Families
The temptation is to spend most of the time on Indo-European because the examples are most accessible for English speakers. Resist this. The student needs depth in at least one non-Indo-European family to understand that phonological diversity extends far beyond what Indo-European languages display. The tonal systems of Niger-Congo, the pharyngeals of Afroasiatic, and the tone sandhi of Sino-Tibetan are essential exposure.

### Not Connecting Sound History to Human History
Grimm's Law and sound correspondences are not just linguistic curiosities -- they are evidence of human migration, cultural contact, and historical change. Every sound correspondence has a story behind it. Make those connections explicit. The student studying language families should be enriching their understanding of civilizational history (1.1) at the same time.
